title: The Healing Practice of Saying Thank You to Money
body:
The Healing Practice of Saying Thank You to Money

Truth is, you probably hate money. Or you fear it. Or you resent it. You might even pretend you don't care about it, which is just another way of saying you're terrified of what it represents in your life. And because of that fear, that resentment, that avoidance, money is probably giving you a lot of grief. It's a vicious cycle, isn't it? You push it away, and then you wonder why it's not showing up for you. You speak ill of it, and then you're surprised when it feels scarce. We've been taught to chase it, hoard it, lament its absence, and worship its presence, but rarely, if ever, to genuinely appreciate it. This isn't just about financial literacy. This is about emotional and spiritual literacy. This is about your deep, often hidden, relationship with one of the most powerful forces in your life.

Your Relationship with Money is a Mess - Let's Fix It

Honestly, let's just admit it. For most of us, our relationship with money is complicated, fraught with anxiety, shame, and judgment. We talk about money like it's a necessary evil, a dirty secret, or an elusive lover who's always just out of reach. We complain about bills, we stress about debt, we envy others' abundance, and we feel guilty about our own. Sound familiar? This isn't just some abstract concept. Your feelings about money are deeply embedded in your psyche. They're tied to your sense of worth, your security, your freedom, and your ability to care for yourself and your loved ones. It's not just numbers in a bank account. It's woven into the fabric of your identity. You feel that?

Think about it. When you get a bill, what's your first reaction? Probably a sigh, a groan, maybe even a flash of anger or dread. When you check your bank balance and it's lower than you'd like, does your stomach drop? Do you start spiraling into fear about the future? That's not a neutral interaction. That's a charged, emotional one. And this constant state of emotional turmoil around money creates a vibration, an energetic signal, that actually repels the very thing you claim to want more of. It's like constantly telling someone you hate them and then wondering why they don't want to spend time with you. It doesn't work that way with people, and it doesn't work that way with money either. Money is just energy with a story attached to it. And the story you're telling yourself, consciously or unconsciously, is shaping your reality.

Lynne Twist, in her powerful book, The Soul of Money, talks about how our culture has instilled three toxic myths about money: scarcity, more is better, and that's just the way it is. These myths permeate our thinking and keep us stuck in a cycle of never-enoughness, always wanting more, and feeling powerless to change our financial circumstances. She argues that until we confront these myths, we'll remain trapped. Vicki Robin, co-author of Your Money or Your Life, similarly challenges us to examine our relationship with money, not just our income and expenses, but our life energy. She asks us to consider what we're truly trading our precious life for. These aren't just financial gurus; they're consciousness teachers who understand that money is a real spiritual tool.

The Dave Ramsey Dilemma: Shame vs. Healing

Now, you might be thinking, "But Kalesh, I need discipline! I need to be told off for my bad habits!" And sure, there's a place for discipline. But often, what's presented as discipline is actually shame. Think about the Dave Ramsey approach. It's very popular, and for some, it works. But for many, it's rooted in a shame-based methodology. It tells you that your debt is a moral failing, that you're stupid for making certain choices, that you need to be "gazelle intense" to escape your "stupidity." Know what I mean? While the intention might be to motivate, the actual impact for many is to deepen the sense of worthlessness and inadequacy around money. It creates more fear, more anxiety, more self-flagellation. And while fear can sometimes be a short-term motivator, it's never a sustainable path to true healing and abundance. It's a quick fix that often leaves deeper wounds unaddressed.

I'm not saying don't pay off your debt. Of course, pay your debt. But the *way* you approach it matters. If you're constantly berating yourself, if you're approaching your financial situation from a place of self-hatred, you're just reinforcing the negative energy around money. Daring Greatly (paid link) by Brené Brown talks about the power of vulnerability and courage, and how shame is a debilitating emotion that keeps us small. You can't shame yourself into a healthy relationship with money any more than you can shame yourself into a healthy relationship with a partner. It just doesn't work. True change comes from a place of self-compassion, understanding, and a willingness to look at the uncomfortable truths without judgment.

Instead of shaming yourself, try this: Notice what your body does when you think about your debt. Does your chest tighten? Do your shoulders hunch? Is there a knot in your stomach? That's your body reacting to the story you're telling yourself. That's the energetic manifestation of your money story. Acknowledging that physical reaction is the first step towards changing it. You can't just ignore your body's signals and expect your mind to magically shift. They're intertwined.

The Radical Act of Gratitude Towards Money

So, if shame isn't the answer, what is? Gratitude. And I know, it sounds almost too simple. Too woo-woo. But hear me out. When you express gratitude for money, you're not just saying "thank you" to a piece of paper or a number on a screen. You're acknowledging the energy it represents. You're acknowledging the service it provides. You're acknowledging the opportunities it creates. You're shifting your energetic vibration from one of lack and fear to one of appreciation and abundance. This isn't about pretending you have more money than you do. It's about appreciating what you *do* have, and what money *does* for you, regardless of the amount.

Think about the money that comes into your life. Your paycheck, a gift, a refund. Instead of immediately thinking about what you "have" to spend it on - bills, debt, necessities - pause. Take a moment. Truly feel gratitude for its arrival. Thank it for the work you did to earn it. Thank it for the security it provides. Thank it for the warmth of your home, the food on your table, the clothes on your back. Even if it's just enough to cover the bare minimum, that's still something to be grateful for. Many people don't even have that.

And here's where it gets really interesting: thank the money that leaves your hands too. Every bill you pay. Every purchase you make. When you pay your rent or mortgage, thank that money for providing you shelter. When you pay your utility bill, thank that money for bringing you light and heat. When you buy groceries, thank that money for nourishing your body. This isn't about being naive. It's about consciously completing the energetic circuit. You're acknowledging that money is a flow, a constant exchange. When you pay a bill with resentment, you're sending out negative energy. When you pay it with gratitude, you're sending out positive energy, and you're signaling to the universe that you appreciate the flow, and you're open to receiving more.

Sadhguru often speaks about how everything in existence is energy. Your thoughts are energy. Your emotions are energy. Money, too, is energy. If you constantly think and feel negatively about money, you're literally pushing that energy away. But if you cultivate a sense of appreciation and reverence, you create an inviting space for that energy to flow towards you. It's not magic in the sense of "poof, a million dollars appears." It's magic in the sense of shifting your internal state to align with what you desire, thereby opening pathways in your external reality.

Your Bank Account is a Mirror, Not a Scorecard

This is a crucial distinction. We often look at our bank balance as a judgment on our worth, our intelligence, our success. If it's high, we feel good about ourselves. If it's low, we feel like failures. But your bank account is simply a reflection of your past choices, your current habits, and your underlying beliefs about money. It's a mirror showing you what's going on inside, not a scorecard ranking your value as a human being. Does that land?

If you look in a mirror and don't like what you see, you don't blame the mirror, do you? You change something about yourself. You change your clothes, you fix your hair, you adjust your posture. It's the same with your bank account. If you don't like what you see, don't blame the numbers. Don't blame the economy. Don't blame your boss. Look inward. What beliefs are you holding? What stories are you telling yourself? What emotional patterns are you repeating? Your bank account is simply reflecting those back to you.

This perspective shift is incredibly liberating. It takes the shame out of the equation. If your account is low, it's not a sign that you're a bad person. It's a signal. A signal that something in your relationship with money needs attention. Maybe it's your spending habits, maybe it's your earning potential, maybe it's a deep-seated belief that you don't deserve abundance. Whatever it is, the mirror is simply showing you where to focus your healing energy. It's an invitation, not a condemnation. And this is where the practice of gratitude becomes so powerful. It allows you to look at that mirror with compassion, rather than criticism.

Consider the work of Brené Brown again, particularly in her book Rising Strong (paid link). She talks about how we can't bypass pain or discomfort. We have to lean into it, learn from it, and then rise from it. Your financial struggles are not something to be avoided or ignored. They are opportunities for real self-discovery and growth. They're a chance to understand your deepest fears and transform them. And you can't do that if you're constantly shaming yourself or blaming the external circumstances.

Practical Steps to Cultivate Money Gratitude

Okay, so how do you actually do this? It's not just about saying "thank you" once and expecting miracles. It's a practice, a daily cultivation. Here are some concrete ways to integrate money gratitude into your life:

  1. The Daily Money Thank You: Every day, find at least one interaction with money to be grateful for. This could be receiving money, spending money, or even just seeing money. "Thank you, money, for my delicious coffee." "Thank you, money, for paying my phone bill and connecting me to the world." "Thank you, money, for the roof over my head." It sounds simple, but consistency is key.
  2. Gratitude for Incoming Money: When you receive any money - a paycheck, a gift, a refund, a found coin - pause. Take a deep breath. Place your hand over your heart and silently (or audibly) say, "Thank you. I appreciate you. I welcome you." Feel the warmth of that gratitude. Don't immediately jump to what you "have" to do with it. Just appreciate its arrival.
  3. Blessing Your Outgoing Money: This is a big one. Before you pay a bill, before you make a purchase, hold the bill or your card (energetically or physically) and say, "Thank you for the service you provided. I bless you and release you to do good in the world." Imagine that money flowing out with positive energy, creating value for others, and then returning to you multiplied. This transforms the act of paying bills from a chore into a conscious act of giving and receiving.
  4. The Money Journal: Keep a small journal specifically for your money experiences. Don't just track numbers. Track your feelings. Write down moments when you felt abundant, even if it was just finding a dollar on the street. Write down what you're grateful for money-wise each day. This builds a positive money story over time.
  5. Acknowledge Your Effort: When you get paid, don't just thank the money. Thank yourself for the effort, skill, and time you invested to earn it. Acknowledge your own worth and contribution. This builds self-esteem and strengthens your deservingness of abundance.
  6. Challenge Negative Self-Talk: When you catch yourself saying or thinking something negative about money ("I never have enough," "Money is hard to get," "I hate paying bills"), gently interrupt that thought. Replace it with a grateful statement. "I am always supported," "Money flows to me easily," "I am grateful for the services I receive." This is a practice of mental discipline.

This isn't about ignoring financial planning or responsible budgeting. Those are still essential. But they become so much easier, so much more effective, when they're approached from a place of gratitude and self-compassion, rather than fear and self-reproach. As Vicki Robin would say, it's about aligning your money with your values, and gratitude helps you clarify those values.

And speaking of aligning money with values, consider the insights in The Art of Money (paid link). It's not just about budgets; it's about understanding your money story, healing old wounds, and creating a conscious relationship with your finances. It's about bringing your whole self to the money conversation, not just the logical, numbers-focused part of you.

The Energetic Ripple Effect

When you start practicing gratitude towards money, you're doing more than just feeling good. You're literally changing your energetic signature. You're telling the universe, "I appreciate what I have. I am open to receiving more." This isn't some magical manifestation trick, it's a fundamental principle of energy. Like attracts like. If you're constantly radiating scarcity and fear, you'll attract more situations that reinforce that. If you're radiating gratitude and appreciation, you'll attract more opportunities for abundance.

This isn't about getting rich quick. It's about building a sustainable, healthy, and conscious relationship with money that supports your life and your purpose. It's about transforming your inner world so that your outer world can reflect that change. It's a journey, not a destination. There will be days when it's hard, days when fear creeps back in. That's okay. Acknowledge it, and then gently redirect back to gratitude. The more you practice, the stronger this muscle becomes.

Think about the freedom that comes from not constantly being at war with your money. Imagine the peace of mind when you can look at your bank account, not with dread, but with a calm understanding that it's just a snapshot, and you have the power to influence that picture. This healing practice isn't just for your bank balance; it's for your soul. It's for your peace. It's for your freedom.

FAQ

How is this different from "manifesting" or "the law of attraction"?

While there are overlaps, this practice is less about wishful thinking and more about conscious relationship building. It's not just about "thinking positive" to get things. It's about actively shifting your emotional and energetic relationship with money. It acknowledges that money is a tool and a form of energy, and your interactions with it impact its flow in your life. It combines internal work (gratitude, healing) with external action (responsible financial management) rather than relying solely on magical thinking.

What if I'm in deep debt and feel like there's nothing to be grateful for?

This is precisely when gratitude is most crucial. Even in deep debt, you still have shelter, food, clothing, clean water, or access to services that money provided. Start there. Be grateful for the basic necessities that money helped provide, even if you're currently paying for them. Be grateful for the ability to earn money, no matter how small the amount. Be grateful for the lessons learned from your debt. Gratitude isn't about ignoring your problems; it's about finding the light even in the darkness, which then gives you the strength and clarity to address those problems more effectively.

Will this actually make me more money?

Directly? No, saying "thank you" to money doesn't magically deposit funds into your account. Indirectly? Absolutely. When you shift from a fearful, scarcity mindset to an abundant, grateful one, you become more open to opportunities. You make clearer decisions. You attract positive interactions. You reduce stress, which frees up mental and emotional energy to pursue income-generating activities. You stop self-sabotaging. It's not a magic bullet, but it's a powerful catalyst for positive financial change.

Your challenge today is simple, but real. The next time any money comes into your possession - a paycheck, a refund, even finding a penny on the sidewalk - pause. For just five seconds. Look at it. Feel it. And silently, or out loud, say "Thank you." Don't judge it. Don't overthink it. Just express genuine gratitude. See what shifts inside you. It's a tiny step, but every healing journey begins with one.

Om Shanti Shanti Shanti
meta_description: Transform your relationship with money. Learn how gratitude can heal financial stress and attract abundance into your life. Discover a new perspective on wealth
author: Kalesh
